concepts.title: Differentiation of Determinants
concepts.conceptBody:
Differentiation of Determinants

Differentiation of a function given in form of a determinant $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{y}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{u}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{v}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{w}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \lambda(\mathrm{x}) & \mu(\mathrm{x}) & \gamma(\mathrm{x}) \end{array}\right| \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{u}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{v}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{w}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \lambda(\mathrm{x}) & \mu(\mathrm{x}) & \gamma(\mathrm{x}) \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{u}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{v}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{w}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \mathrm{p}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{q}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{r}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \lambda(\mathrm{x}) & \mu(\mathrm{x}) & \gamma(\mathrm{x}) \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{u}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{v}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{w}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x}) \\ \lambda^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mu^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \gamma^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \end{array}\right| \end{aligned} $$ The differentiation can also be done by column-wise.

examples.title: If $f_n(x), g_n(x), h_n(x), n=1,2,3$ are polynomials in $x$ such that $f_n(a)=g_n(a)=h_n(a), n=1,2,3$ and $F(x)=\left|\begin{array}{lll}f_1(x) & f_2(x) & f_3(x) \\ g_1(x) & g_2(x) & g_3(x) \\ h_1(x) & h_2(x) & h_3(x)\end{array}\right| . \quad$ Then $F^{\prime}(a)$ is equal to
Let $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x^3 & \sin x & \cos x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^2 & p^3\end{array}\right|$, where $p$ is a constant. Then $\frac{d^3}{d x^3}\{f(x)\}$ at $x=0$ is
$f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x^3 & x^2 & 3 x^2 \\ 1 & -6 & 4 \\ p & p^2 & p^3\end{array}\right|$, here $p$ is a constant, then $\frac{d^3 f(x)}{d x^3}$ is
